description: An infectious agent 'sewaro' when it passes between hosts, reservoirs, or environments along a pathway. Transmission may occur before a person feels ill, and contact alone may end without it. 'perawi' (expose) names contact with the agent; 'peshiro' (infection) begins when the agent establishes itself in or on a host.
articulatory_notes: Send unvoiced air through a narrow groove along the tongue for 's', then move into true-mid front unrounded 'e'. Round the lips and raise the back of the tongue for voiced 'w', then open into stressed central 'a'. Make a tap or short trill with the tongue tip and draw the tongue back into true-mid rounded back 'o'.
sound_symbolism: The medial glide and final tongue turn give the word a felt path through the mouth. That travelling contour offers a mnemonic for an agent passing along a route.
usage_notes: Transitive use follows an agent passing something to a host; intransitive use keeps the process itself in view. A 'thue' phrase can introduce the pathway. The same form names the event, transmission.

examples.phi: ha peshiro thue phialu po sewaro.
examples.translation: The infection may transmit through water.

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Tracing the agent and its pathway supports prevention without treating an infected person as impure or blameworthy.

semantic_domains.nature: movement of an infectious agent through a biological pathway
semantic_domains.physical: passage between hosts, reservoirs, or environments
